channel.title: NYT > Arts > Art & Design
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The Venice Biennale and Its Many Flashpoints: An Explainer
item.description: The sudden death of a curator, the surprise participation of Russia and an unconventional U.S. entry are heating up the art world Olympics, which begins previews Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The central pavilion of the Giardini on Tuesday, first day of previews for the 61st Venice Biennale. The exhibition, “In Minor Keys,” was initiated by Koyo Kouoh and overseen by her team after her death last year.

item.title: With Free Vodka, and a Few Protests, Russia Returns to the Venice Biennale
item.description: On Tuesday, Russia opened a pavilion at the Venice Biennale for the first since its full-scale invasion of Ukraine. Ukraine’s own exhibit is just a stone’s throw.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: Venice Biennale: In Photos and Video
item.description: Scenes from the highly anticipated art event.
item.dc:creator: Matteo de Mayda
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times

item.title: Francisco de Zurbarán: Paintings So Real, You Can Hardly Resist Believing
item.description: An exhibition of works by Francisco de Zurbarán at London’s National Gallery highlights the painter’s ability to draw in the viewer with detail and drama.
item.dc:creator: Emily LaBarge
item.media:description: “The Crucifixion” (1627) by Francisco de Zurbarán opens the exhibition.

item.title: Venice Biennale Jury Resigns Amid Tension Over Awards Ban
item.description: The jury of the world’s most important art exhibition had said it wouldn’t consider artists from countries whose leaders are accused of crimes against humanity.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: The Israel pavilion at the Venice Biennale in 2024.

item.title: A Curator Died Suddenly. Could the Venice Biennale Be Saved?
item.description: Koyo Kouoh, who died of cancer at 57, was just months into her dream job overseeing the Venice Biennale’s centerpiece exhibition. But she left a plan that her assistants have tried to realize.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.category: Zeitz Museum of Contemporary Art Africa
item.media:credit: Tsele Nthane for The New York Times
item.media:description: Koyo Kouoh in South Africa in 2023. Her husband said that curating the Venice Biennale’s centerpiece exhibition was Kouoh’s ultimate goal.

item.title: The Prince of Italian Pop Art Smiles Again
item.description: Lévy Gorvy Dayan gallery offers new glimpses of the perfectionism and hidden goofiness of Domenico Gnoli, whose caricatures of reality captured la dolce vita.
item.dc:creator: Walker Mimms

item.title: With ‘Sister Dreamer’ Park, Lauren Halsey Brings Her Heroes Home
item.description: The artist’s installation in South Central Los Angeles elevates familiar faces in the neighborhood with monuments of stone.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin and Amanda Hakan
item.media:credit: Amanda Hakan for The New York Times

item.title: U.S. and Italy Honor Alliance to Curb Art Looting, Amid Broader Tensions
item.description: Standing near dozens of repatriated artifacts, officials from the countries celebrated efforts that have led to the return of thousands of artworks to Italy.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo

item.title: $100 Million Award Made in Suit Over Unlicensed Robert Indiana Art
item.description: A New York jury found that an art publisher who made works derived from Indiana’s images had infringed on the rights of a company that had been the artist’s partner.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:description: Robert Indiana’s most famous work, “LOVE.” There are iterations of the sculpture in several cities, including this one at John F. Kennedy Plaza in Philadelphia.

item.title: This ‘Sustainable’ Island Is Venice’s Newest Art Oasis
item.description: Patrizia Sandretto Re Rebaudengo has turned a former gunpowder store into a showcase for her contemporary art collection.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Installing Pamela Rosenkranz’s “Old Tree (Pink Seas)” on the island of San Giacomo in Paludo, in Venice.

item.title: $450 Million Worth of Newhouse Trophies Come to Christie’s
item.description: A special May evening sale will feature 16 artworks from the museum-quality collection of the Condé Nast chief S.I. Newhouse Jr.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Succession Brancusi/All rights reserved (Adagp); Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Brancusi head in the collection of S.I. Newhouse Jr., expected to sell for $100 million, is decorated with gold leaf.

item.title: How a Museum Doubled Its Attendance in Just One Year
item.description: The Museum of the Moving Image in Queens, long a home for cinephiles, found success, and new visitors, by repositioning itself as a community hub.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik and Gus Aronson

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in April
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Max Lakin covers N. Dash’s mysterious scraps of cotton, Vaclav Pozarek’s spare, witty sculptures and Martin Wong’s Popeyes.
item.dc:creator: Max Lakin, Seph Rodney, Andrew Russeth, Jillian Steinhauer and Travis Diehl

item.title: For an Indiana Couple, This Schoolhouse Became Their Home
item.description: Stacie Grissom and Sean Wilson’s 9,000-square-foot Indiana schoolhouse is a lesson in courage.
item.dc:creator: Lia Picard and Michelle Litvin
item.media:description: Stacie Grissom and Sean Wilson with their children. The couple renovated the former Union Joint schoolhouse in Franklin, Ind., with a sense of whimsy.

item.title: A Loft with Past Lives Gets an Owner Making a New One
item.description: How Jessica Helfand, an artist, started a new chapter in an industrial building.
item.dc:creator: Diana Budds and Jane Beiles
item.media:description: Jessica Helfand in her studio in the repurposed loft. The studio benefits from indirect light streaming in throughout the day.

item.title: Donlyn Lyndon, Last Surviving Creator of the Sea Ranch, Dies at 90
item.description: With his classmates Charles Moore, William Turnbull Jr. and Richard Whitaker, he designed an icon of modern architecture on a windswept bluff in Northern California.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: Inside the Met Gala After-Parties: See the Stars and Their Looks
item.description: Models, designers, D.J.s and performers kept the night going into the early morning.
item.dc:creator: Jacob Bernstein and Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Rebecca Smeyne for The New York Times

item.title: A Forum for African Voices Draws Inspiration From a Former Champion
item.description: The African Art in Venice Forum is continuing its mission of addressing the continent’s artistic needs, even as it mourns the loss of Koyo Kouoh, a key supporter.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.category: African Art in Venice Forum (African Art Dialogues)

item.title: Venice Showcases an Array of Global Art Stars During the Biennale
item.description: Marina Abramovic, Lorna Simpson, Arthur Jafa, Richard Prince and Peggy Guggenheim are among those being featured at museums and foundations during this year’s international art exhibition.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: A Dutch Art Studio Lights Up Venice’s Grand Canal
item.description: Lonneke Gordijn and Ralph Nauta of Studio Drift have brought their kinetic light sculptures outdoors at the Venice Biennale.
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal

item.title: For the Painter Michael Armitage, Art Comes With a ‘Sense of Responsibility’
item.description: The Kenyan British artist Michael Armitage, who is showing his paintings and studies in Venice, reflects on his Kenyan roots and his recent move to Indonesia.
item.dc:creator: Farah Nayeri

item.title: At 90, the Korean artist Lee Ufan Finds Energy in His Art
item.description: The Korean artist Lee Ufan continues to work every day, and has three exhibitions on the horizon, including a display of his pivotal paintings at the Venice Biennale.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Russeth and Matteo de Mayda
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee has painted two radiant little pieces on a floor and a wall at the San Marco Art Centre in Venice as part of his exhibition there during the Venice Biennale.

item.title: In Venice, an Ocean-Inspired Exhibition Takes Visitors Under the Sea
item.description: Inside a former convent on an island, immersive artworks invite visitors to “stretch their sense of self to include the vastness of the ocean.”
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: A Show in Rome Sheds New Light on Francesca Woodman’s Haunting Images
item.description: The American photographer died at just 22, and yet the beautiful, unsettling work she created continues to influence art and artists. Why?
item.dc:creator: Dale Berning Sawa

item.title: In Venice, Meditating on Time and Place on a Leisurely Bar Crawl
item.description: “You can’t escape drink in Venice,” one patron noted. “It’s a ritual.” An adopted Venetian, a reporter and a photographer wandered the city’s watering holes over one long aperitivo hour.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Matteo de Mayda for The New York Times
item.media:description: A view of the San Basegio Canal in Venice. The island city has retained a highly distinctive bar culture.

item.title: This Professor Won the ‘Bauhaus Bathroom’ Design Competition for the Gropius House
item.description: Announcing the winner of a competition to create a public restroom at the historic Gropius House.
item.dc:creator: Jane Margolies

item.title: Nicole Hollander, Acerbic Feminist Cartoonist, Dies at 86
item.description: For more than 30 years, she wrote and illustrated “Sylvia,” a comic strip about a tart-tongued, witty woman unafraid of expressing her many opinions.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: How 7 Looks for ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ Came Together
item.description: The film’s costume designer, Molly Rogers, who also worked on the original, shared the inspirations for some of her favorite designs.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It was like coming back to summer camp,” said Molly Rogers, who took over as the lead costume designer on the sequel after working as associate designer on the original “Devil Wears Prada.”

item.title: Stephanie Chernikowski, 84, Dies; Photographed the ‘Rough Magic’ of Punk
item.description: She captured the radiant chaos at New York City clubs like CBGB in the 1970s, when bands like the Ramones, Blondie and the Patti Smith Group were emerging.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: This Dance Duo Gets Down to Business, With a Dose of the Erotic
item.description: Bullyache’s show “A Good Man Is Hard to Find,” about the violence of corporate life, combines choreography and theater with pop culture verve.
item.dc:creator: Emily J. May and Sophie Stafford
item.media:credit: Sophie Stafford for The New York Times

item.title: Gerry Conway, a Creator of the Punisher in Spider-Man Comics, Dies at 73
item.description: The brutal assassin’s skull logo later took on new life as a political symbol. Mr. Conway was also responsible for the death of Gwen Stacy, Peter Parker’s love interest.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines
item.media:credit: Gabe Ginsberg/Getty Images for Amazing Comic Conventions
item.media:description: Gerry Conway in 2019. When Marvel killed off Gwen Stacy, Mr. Conway recalled, “I was the most hated man in comic books.”

item.title: The Audacity of Art at the Obama Presidential Center
item.description: Barack and Michelle Obama commissioned 30 artists to create work for their campus, which starts visitor previews next week on the South Side of Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin and Kevin Serna
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum

item.title: New Banksy Statue Causes Stir in Central London
item.description: The statue depicts a man marching with a flag that covers his face. It appeared in a section of London near statues of 19th-century British military and colonial figures.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:description: The artist Banksy appeared to have taken credit for statue of a man holding a flag covering his face that stood in Waterloo Place in London.

item.title: Georg Baselitz, German Neo-Expressionist Painter, Dies at 88
item.description: Along with contemporaries like Anselm Kiefer, he mounted a frontal attack on Minimalism and Conceptualism, the dominant “cool” styles of the 1970s.
item.dc:creator: William Grimes

item.title: Nine Artists on the Gardens They’ll Never Forget
item.description: Hiroshi Sugimoto, Irene Neuwirth, Umberto Pasti and others share their impressions of mesmerizing plots all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Michael Snyder

item.title: Unexpected Mother’s Day Gift Ideas
item.description: Blossom-filled books, welcoming bird houses and more recommendations from T Magazine.
